text: The surgeon performs a dissection from medial to lateral above the sacral promontory, initially bridging the peritoneum. The inferior mesenteric artery is dissected from its attachments, cleared, and secured with three hemolog clips: two on the proximal side and one on the distal specimen side.
The surgeon detaches Girota's fascia from the colon, continuing the dissection from medial to lateral.

The surgeon continues the dissection towards the DJ flexure, carefully identifying the inferior mesenteric vein near this anatomical landmark. The vein is dissected from surrounding tissues and clipped using hemlock clips, with two clips placed on the proximal side and one on the distal side.
The surgeon carefully navigates near the tail of the pancreas after dissecting the inferior mesenteric vein, with the option to enter the lesser sac visible in the surgical field.
A swab is placed over the surgical area, and the dissection proceeds from the lateral to medial side, joining the previously dissected medial to lateral section.
The surgeon detaches the gastrocolic ligament from the colon, providing access to the lesser sac and facilitating the complete mobilization of the splenic flexure.
The surgeon continues dissection into the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane posteriorly, using a swab for retraction to avoid breaching the TME package, ensuring the integrity of the specimen.
The surgeon continues dissection posteriorly and laterally, carefully avoiding nerve damage, while also advancing anteriorly. The narrow pelvis is visible, and the specimen is detached laterally.

The surgeon completes the dissection by joining the dissected areas from all sides, with the distal margin at the pelvic floor. Anteriorly, careful dissection is performed to avoid damaging the prostate or seminal vesicles, while the fascia of Denonvilliers is visible. Suction is used to clear smoke and maintain a clear view in the narrow pelvis.

The surgical field shows minimal blood loss as the surgeon prepares the area for transection at the pelvic level. A sigmoidoscopy is performed to determine the transection level, followed by the use of a 45mm Endo GIA stapler twice to complete the transection.

The surgeon retrieves a large specimen from the pelvis, revealing a clean surgical field with intact nerves and presacral fascia. The staple line is visible, and the seminal vesicles remain undamaged. An end-to-end coloanal anastomosis is performed using a circular stapler, followed by the creation of a proximal ileostomy.
